title: Grupera

It may include [hallucinated](/wiki/Hallucination_(artificial_intelligence) "Hallucination (artificial intelligence)") information, [copyright violations](/wiki/Wikipedia:COPYVIO "Wikipedia:COPYVIO"), claims not [verified](/wiki/Wikipedia:V "Wikipedia:V") in cited sources, [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:OR "Wikipedia:OR"), or [fictitious references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Fictitious_references "Wikipedia:Fictitious references"). Any such material should be [removed](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Grupera&action=edit). *(August 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

* [Vocals](/wiki/Singing "Singing") * [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") * [electric bass](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar") * [keyboards](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard") * [drums](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit") * [percussion](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument")

(also known as **Grupero** or **Onda Grupera**) is a subgenre of [regional Mexican](/wiki/Regional_Mexican "Regional Mexican") music. It reached the height of its popularity in the 1990s, especially in [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural") areas.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Grupera music traces its roots to the [rock groups](/wiki/Rock_group "Rock group") of the 1960s, yet has evolved significantly over time. Typically performed by ensembles of four or more musicians, the genre prominently features [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"), [electronic keyboards](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard"), and [drums](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit"). The popularity of grupera music surged notably during the 1980s, propelling it into commercial viability and establishing its place within the [Latin music](/wiki/Latin_music "Latin music") landscape. Today, the genre is widely acknowledged and celebrated in prestigious Latin music awards ceremonies such as [Lo Nuestro Awards](/wiki/Lo_Nuestro_Awards "Lo Nuestro Awards") and the [Latin Grammy Awards](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Awards "Latin Grammy Awards"), reflecting its enduring influence and cultural significance across Latin America and beyond. Grupera music is a distinct Mexican musical genre that blends traditional [ranchera](/wiki/Ranchera "Ranchera") melodies with influences from [norteño](/wiki/Norte%C3%B1o_(music) "Norteño (music)") and other regional styles like [corridos](/wiki/Corrido "Corrido"), [cumbias](/wiki/Cumbia "Cumbia"), [charangas](/wiki/Charanga-vallenata "Charanga-vallenata"), [ballads](/wiki/Sentimental_ballad "Sentimental ballad"), [boleros](/wiki/Bolero#Cuba "Bolero") and [huapangos](/wiki/Huapango "Huapango").
Mary Farquharson, [Mexico](https://books.google.com/books?id=QzX8THIgRjUC&dq=grupera+mexico&pg=PA472). *The Rough Guide to World Music*, Rough Guides, 2000, p. 472.

["Diferencia entre ser ranchero y ser grupero"](https://www.soygrupero.com.mx/mundo-grupero/diferencia-entre-ser-ranchero-y-ser-grupero/2019/04/). *Soy Grupero: ¡Lo mejor del mundo grupero! Entérate ahora*. 12 April 2019. Retrieved 22 March 2020.

["Canal Once - México Grupero"](https://web.archive.org/web/20180123134757/http://oncetv-ipn.net/mexicogrupero/capitulos.htm). *oncetv-ipn.net* (in Spanish). Archived from [the original](http://oncetv-ipn.net/mexicogrupero/capitulos.htm) on 23 January 2018. Retrieved 22 March 2020.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:OOjs_UI_icon_robot.svg) | This article **may incorporate text from a [large language model](/wiki/Wikipedia:LLM "Wikipedia:LLM")**. It may include [hallucinated](/wiki/Hallucination_(artificial_intelligence) "Hallucination (artificial intelligence)") information, [copyright violations](/wiki/Wikipedia:COPYVIO "Wikipedia:COPYVIO"), claims not [verified](/wiki/Wikipedia:V "Wikipedia:V") in cited sources, [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:OR "Wikipedia:OR"), or [fictitious references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Fictitious_references "Wikipedia:Fictitious references"). Any such material should be [removed](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Grupera&action=edit). *(August 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Genre of Regional Mexican music

| Grupera | |
| --- | --- |
| [Ana Bárbara](/wiki/Ana_B%C3%A1rbara "Ana Bárbara") singing in concert | |
| Other names | Onda Grupera |
| Stylistic origins | * [Latin rock](/wiki/Latin_rock "Latin rock") * [latin pop](/wiki/Latin_pop "Latin pop") * [mariachi](/wiki/Mariachi "Mariachi") * [cumbia](/wiki/Cumbia "Cumbia") * [bolero](/wiki/Bolero "Bolero") * [norteño](/wiki/Norte%C3%B1o_(music) "Norteño (music)") |
| Cultural origins | Early 1970s, [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") |
| Typical instruments | * [Vocals](/wiki/Singing "Singing") * [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") * [electric bass](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar") * [keyboards](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard") * [drums](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit") * [percussion](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") |

**Grupera** (also known as **Grupero** or **Onda Grupera**) is a subgenre of [regional Mexican](/wiki/Regional_Mexican "Regional Mexican") music. It reached the height of its popularity in the 1990s, especially in [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural") areas.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Grupera music traces its roots to the [rock groups](/wiki/Rock_group "Rock group") of the 1960s, yet has evolved significantly over time. Typically performed by ensembles of four or more musicians, the genre prominently features [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"), [electronic keyboards](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard"), and [drums](/wiki/Drum_kit "Drum kit"). The popularity of grupera music surged notably during the 1980s, propelling it into commercial viability and establishing its place within the [Latin music](/wiki/Latin_music "Latin music") landscape. Today, the genre is widely acknowledged and celebrated in prestigious Latin music awards ceremonies such as [Lo Nuestro Awards](/wiki/Lo_Nuestro_Awards "Lo Nuestro Awards") and the [Latin Grammy Awards](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Awards "Latin Grammy Awards"), reflecting its enduring influence and cultural significance across Latin America and beyond. Grupera music is a distinct Mexican musical genre that blends traditional [ranchera](/wiki/Ranchera "Ranchera") melodies with influences from [norteño](/wiki/Norte%C3%B1o_(music) "Norteño (music)") and other regional styles like [corridos](/wiki/Corrido "Corrido"), [cumbias](/wiki/Cumbia "Cumbia"), [charangas](/wiki/Charanga-vallenata "Charanga-vallenata"), [ballads](/wiki/Sentimental_ballad "Sentimental ballad"), [boleros](/wiki/Bolero#Cuba "Bolero") and [huapangos](/wiki/Huapango "Huapango").

Some of the most recognized groups and artists in the grupera genre include [Los Bukis](/wiki/Los_Bukis "Los Bukis"), [Los Temerarios](/wiki/Los_Temerarios "Los Temerarios"), [Bronco](/wiki/Bronco_(Mexican_band) "Bronco (Mexican band)"), [Los Yonic's](/wiki/Los_Yonic%27s "Los Yonic's"), and [Grupo Bryndis](/wiki/Grupo_Bryndis "Grupo Bryndis"). Grupera music has enjoyed great popularity in Mexico and other parts of [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), and continues to be an important part of Mexican musical culture.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grupera&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The original wave of Mexican rock bands got their start mostly with Spanish covers of popular English rock songs. After this initial stage, they moved on to include in their repertoire traditional [ranchera](/wiki/Ranchera "Ranchera") songs, in addition to [cumbias](/wiki/Cumbia "Cumbia") and [ballads](/wiki/Sentimental_ballad "Sentimental ballad"). Thus, the 1970s saw the rise of a number of grupera bands that specialized in romantic ballads and songs that up to that point had only been sung with [mariachi](/wiki/Mariachi "Mariachi").

The name *grupera* comes from the fact that many of the bands had names starting with *Grupo* (Group).[[2]](#cite_note-RiveraGodina-2)
Most of these groups had a rural origin and many previously played [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"). According to Madrid, cited by Rivera Godina,[[2]](#cite_note-RiveraGodina-2) the grupero movement had its peak in the 1970s with many famous artists who were included under the label [tropical music](/wiki/Tropical_music "Tropical music"), such as Mike Laure, [Rigo Tovar](/wiki/Rigo_Tovar "Rigo Tovar"), [Chico Che](/wiki/Chico_Che "Chico Che"), among others, both Mexican and from other countries, who fused romantic ballads with more danceable rhythms, such as [Los Baby's](/wiki/Los_Baby%27s "Los Baby's"), [Los Freddy's](/wiki/Los_Freddy%27s "Los Freddy's"), Los Corazones Solitarios, [Los Pasteles Verdes](/wiki/Los_Pasteles_Verdes "Los Pasteles Verdes"), [Los Barón de Apodaca](/wiki/Los_Bar%C3%B3n_de_Apodaca "Los Barón de Apodaca"), and [Los Caminantes](/wiki/Los_Caminantes "Los Caminantes").
Under the influence of the music and film industries, the most well-known [norteño](/wiki/Norte%C3%B1o_(music) "Norteño (music)") bands became synonymous with *grupero*.[[3]](#cite_note-Arias-3)

In the 1990s, the term *grupero* and its variations started to be part of Mexico's music industry and began to receive media attention,[[2]](#cite_note-RiveraGodina-2) including different bands such as [Los Temerarios](/wiki/Los_Temerarios "Los Temerarios"), [Los Bukis](/wiki/Los_Bukis "Los Bukis"), [Los Tigres del Norte](/wiki/Los_Tigres_del_Norte "Los Tigres del Norte"), [Bronco](/wiki/Bronco_(Mexican_band) "Bronco (Mexican band)"), [La Mafia](/wiki/La_Mafia "La Mafia"), La Industria del Amor, [Conjunto Primavera](/wiki/Conjunto_Primavera "Conjunto Primavera"), [Ana Bárbara](/wiki/Ana_B%C3%A1rbara "Ana Bárbara"), [Grupo Bryndis](/wiki/Grupo_Bryndis "Grupo Bryndis"), [Los Acosta](/wiki/Los_Acosta "Los Acosta"), Liberación, Grupo Mandigo, Mi Bande El Mexicano, [Banda Machos](/wiki/Banda_Machos "Banda Machos")[[4]](#cite_note-4) and, as the first American artist popular on both sides of the border, [Selena](/wiki/Selena "Selena").
The media industry popularized this term to generate products based on grupera, such as the radio station [XEQR-FM](/wiki/XEQR-FM "XEQR-FM"), whose slogan was *Salvajemente grupera* (*savagely grupero*), specialized magazines like *[Furia Musical](/wiki/Furia_Musical "Furia Musical")* and cable channels like [Bandamax](/wiki/Bandamax "Bandamax").
Enterprises like Televisa included bands from the grupera wave like Bronco in telenovelas such as *[Dos mujeres, un camino](/wiki/Dos_mujeres,_un_camino "Dos mujeres, un camino")*.[[3]](#cite_note-Arias-3)
Later, [tropical](/wiki/Tropical "Tropical") [cumbia](/wiki/Cumbia "Cumbia") artists such as [Celso Piña](/wiki/Celso_Pi%C3%B1a "Celso Piña"), Margarita la Diosa de la Cumbia, and [Los Ángeles Azules](/wiki/Los_%C3%81ngeles_Azules "Los Ángeles Azules") would be included under the term *grupera*.

A turning point in the development of grupera was the popularization of groups based on [banda sinaloense](/wiki/Banda_music "Banda music") mainly through [Banda el Recodo](/wiki/Banda_el_Recodo "Banda el Recodo") and others like La Original Banda El Limón and [La Arrolladora Banda El Limón](/wiki/La_Arrolladora_Banda_El_Lim%C3%B3n "La Arrolladora Banda El Limón").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[3]](#cite_note-Arias-3)

In the early 2000s, in Mexico the term [Regional Mexican](/wiki/Regional_Mexican "Regional Mexican") began to be used. *Regional Mexican music* had already been used for several years in the United States to refer homogeneously to the regional variations of rural Mexican music and avoid including [tropical music](/wiki/Tropical_music "Tropical music") artists under the label. Meanwhile, *grupera* came to refer specifically to the bands and solo artists that use electric guitars and basses, electronic keyboards and drums, but that played [ballads](/wiki/Ballad "Ballad"), [cumbias](/wiki/Cumbia "Cumbia"), [rancheras](/wiki/Ranchera "Ranchera"), [corridos](/wiki/Corrido "Corrido"), [boleros](/wiki/Bolero "Bolero") and [huapangos](/wiki/Huapango "Huapango"), with said genre being one of many styles under the Regional Mexican umbrella.

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grupera&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Awards")]

* [Latin Grammy Award for Best Grupero Album](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Award_for_Best_Grupero_Album "Latin Grammy Award for Best Grupero Album")
